pass1_property_insights: Private connectivity is already in use across the tenant (56 private endpoints and 74 private DNS zones with 72 VNet links): this shows an established pattern of using Private Endpoint + Private DNS for service isolation, so plan the finance web app and its Azure SQL backend to use Private Endpoints and private DNS zone links rather than public endpoints.

SQL servers show mixed public access (≈57% Enabled, ≈43% Disabled) with minimal TLS 1.2 enforced on most servers: because there is partial adoption of network-restricted SQL, design the finance database to have publicNetworkAccess disabled and require TLS1.2 to meet the tenant's stronger security posture.

Azure SQL databases are concentrated in eastus (80%) and commonly sized as Hyperscale (60% by tier): given the regional and SKU patterns, place the finance database in eastus and select a GeneralPurpose or Hyperscale tier based on expected data size and growth to align with existing operational region and scaling conventions.

Web hosting in the tenant commonly uses App Service Basic SKUs and public access (many serverfarms are B1 and web/sites show publicNetworkAccess Enabled): since internal finance apps need to be internal-only, plan to host the app either behind an internal Application Gateway/WAF or use App Service/Container Apps with publicNetworkAccess disabled (or ASE/ILB deployment) to avoid exposing the app publicly.

WAF-enabled application gateways and Front Door WAF are in use (application gateways all WAF_v2 and Front Door Premium_AzureFrontDoor prevalent): because tenant practice includes WAF protection, put a WAF (internal App Gateway or Front Door where appropriate) in front of the finance web app for consistent threat protection.

Key Vaults almost universally have soft-delete enabled (≈98.8%) but only ≈8.9% have purge protection and ≈39.6% use RBAC: given the tenant's partial hardening of vaults, enforce RBAC, enable purge protection, and restrict Key Vault network access for finance secrets to meet a higher compliance/security bar.

User-assigned managed identities are widely present (370 identities): since the tenant commonly uses UAMIs for service identity, plan to assign a user-assigned managed identity to the web app for DB and Key Vault access to reuse established identity patterns and simplify permission management.

Virtual networks frequently include named subnets for apps and endpoints (default present in ≈40.5% of VNets, appSubnet ≈10.7%, endpointSubnet ≈10.7%) and NSGs are ubiquitous (957 NSGs, heavy eastus footprint): therefore, place the app and database endpoints into appropriately named subnets and use NSGs to restrict lateral access (allow only required ports and management sources).

Backups and recovery vaults are common (90 Recovery Services vaults, RS0 dominant): because tenant practice includes recovery services, ensure the finance database backup/retention strategy integrates with tenant backup conventions (or document equivalence if using Azure SQL automated backups) and register recovery vaults as needed for compliance.

Monitoring and telemetry are widely deployed (549 Application Insights components and 149 Log Analytics workspaces with ingestion/query access enabled): to match tenant telemetry patterns, include Application Insights and send diagnostics to a Log Analytics workspace, but validate network or private-link access if telemetry must be restricted.

Storage accounts are typically Standard LRS with permissive network defaults (Standard_LRS ≈48.5% and network defaultAction Allow ≈92%): because the tenant currently uses permissive storage ACLs, tighten storage network rules for any finance-related storage (deny by default, use private endpoints, enforce TLS1.2) to reduce data-exposure risk.
pass2_mfi_supplementary: Web apps commonly co-occur with private endpoints, private DNS zones, serverfarms and App Service certificates (MFI: microsoft.network/networksecuritygroups, microsoft.network/privatednszones, microsoft.network/privateendpoints, microsoft.network/virtualnetworks, microsoft.web/certificates, microsoft.web/serverfarms, microsoft.web/sites — support 0.0408, reliability medium): this indicates tenant practice of internal App Service deployments that also manage TLS via App Service certificate resources and private DNS, so plan to provision certificate lifecycle (App Service Certificates or Key Vault-backed certificates) and the matching private DNS records alongside the app's private endpoint to maintain internal TLS continuity and automated renewals.

Web hosting often appears together with Key Vault, Application Insights and a Log Analytics workspace (MFI: microsoft.insights/components, microsoft.keyvault/vaults, microsoft.operationalinsights/workspaces, microsoft.web/serverfarms, microsoft.web/sites — support 0.0224, reliability medium): this shows a pattern of pairing secure secret management and centralized telemetry with App Service deployments, so plan to integrate the finance app with Application Insights and Log Analytics and grant the app's identity least-privileged access to Key Vault (and any diagnostic sinks) to enable secure, instrumented operations.

Application Gateways in the tenant frequently co-occur with WAF policies, NSGs and public IPs (MFI: microsoft.network/applicationgateways, microsoft.network/applicationgatewaywebapplicationfirewallpolicies, microsoft.network/networksecuritygroups, microsoft.network/publicipaddresses, microsoft.network/virtualnetworks — support 0.0245, reliability medium): this reflects a tendency to front web apps with internet-facing WAF-backed gateways and perimeter controls, so for the internal-only finance app explicitly choose an internal (ILB) Application Gateway or ensure the gateway is deployed without a public IP and with NSG/firewall policies to prevent inadvertent public exposure.

Key Vaults regularly appear with user-assigned managed identities and network security perimeter resources (including Traffic Manager in some patterns) (MFI: microsoft.keyvault/vaults, microsoft.managedidentity/userassignedidentities, microsoft.network/networksecurityperimeters, microsoft.network/trafficmanagerprofiles, microsoft.network/virtualnetworks — support 0.0286, reliability medium): this suggests tenants rely on managed identities for vault access and sometimes global routing/HA, so if you plan multi-region or failover for the finance application ensure Key Vault access (private endpoints, firewall rules, RBAC) and managed-identity permissions are configured to support cross-region secret/certificate retrieval and automated rotation without breaking failover workflows.
